one_line_takeaway: A conceptual survey arguing that persistent LLM-agent memory is a new security substrate, offering a six-phase memory-lifecycle taxonomy and a formal five-primitive Verifiable Memory Governance framework, with provenance infrastructure identified as the missing foundation.
key_finding: Defenses are unevenly distributed across the memory lifecycle: threats at the WRITE and RETRIEVE phases are comparatively well-studied, while defenses at STORE, SHARE, and FORGET & ROLLBACK remain sparse, and no existing benchmark covers the full memory lifecycle. The VMG dependency analysis yields the central diagnostic: Provenance Visibility (PV) -- which underpins both Rollbackability and Verified Forgetting -- is rare in published architectures, so near-term progress should prioritize building provenance infrastructure before pursuing higher-level primitives whose architectural preconditions are absent. Overall thesis: LTM security cannot be retrofitted at retrieval or execution time alone but must be anchored in storage-time provenance, versioning, and policy-aware retention from the outset.
core_contribution: A survey (SoK-style) of security for writable, cross-session Long-Term Memory (LTM) in LLM agents. Its own contributions are conceptual, not empirical: (1) a Memory Lifecycle Framework that organizes attacks and defenses along two axes -- six lifecycle phases (WRITE, STORE, RETRIEVE, EXECUTE, SHARE & PROPAGATE, FORGET & ROLLBACK) and four security objectives (Integrity, Confidentiality, Availability, Governance) -- to surface cross-phase attack chains that single-turn or input-centric frameworks miss; and (2) Verifiable Memory Governance (VMG), a formal framework of five architectural primitives (Write Authorization, Provenance Visibility, Principal-Scoped Retrieval, Rollbackability, Verified Forgetting), each given a predicate definition over a memory store M_t and a proposed evaluation metric. The paper argues LTM introduces a qualitatively new threat substrate with three properties -- persistence, statefulness, and propagation -- not captured by prompt-injection or RAG-corruption models, and that robust LTM security must be anchored in storage-time provenance, versioning, and policy-aware retention rather than retrofitted at retrieval/execution time.
threat_model: Adversary against an LLM agent with writable, retrievable, cross-session persistent memory. Rather than influencing a single response, the attacker seeks to manipulate how the system remembers, retrieves, plans, and acts across future sessions. The survey organizes write-path attackers by decreasing privilege: corpus write access, single/query-only interaction, and environment-only influence (e.g., poisoning a web page the agent merely browses). Three defining properties distinguish LTM threats: persistence (a poisoned entry recallable across indefinitely many future sessions), statefulness (behavioral drift from accumulated biased memories before any single entry trips a safety classifier), and propagation (contamination spreading laterally agent-to-agent, vertically user-to-organization, and temporally session-to-session via shared stores, inter-agent messages, and tool arguments). A key structural insight is the cross-phase attack chain: poison seeded at WRITE lies dormant in STORE, reactivates at RETRIEVE, steers behavior at EXECUTE, spreads via SHARE, and survives incomplete cleanup at FORGET -- so single-turn detection cannot observe both ends of the chain.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) the field is fast-moving, so the taxonomy may not capture recent/unpublished work, especially undocumented industrial memory systems; (2) the Memory Lifecycle Framework is an organizational scaffold, not a strict decomposition -- phase boundaries blur (retrieval/execution coupling, continuous storage/summarization, implicit sharing via tool calls/logs), so some attacks/defenses span multiple phases. Reviewer-added: as a survey it presents no original empirical validation of the framework or VMG primitives; the VMG metrics are proposed but not measured; and the quantitative attack figures it cites are drawn entirely from other papers, not established here.

datasets_used: None -- this is a literature survey with no original experiments or datasets. MemoryOS appears only as a cited attack target (InjecMEM reports 76.6% ASR on it), not as data used by the authors.
quantitative_results: Framework scale: six lifecycle phases x four security objectives; five VMG primitives (all authors' own structural counts).

Table 1: compares this survey against nine prior surveys along three dimensions (LifeCycle, Benign Risk, Conceptual Framework); the paper claims prior surveys address at most one axis partially while this work addresses all three (authors' own comparison).
CITED (from other works, not the survey's experiments) -- AgentPoison (Chen et al., 2024): corpus-level embedding-space backdoor with <0.1% poison rate and >=80% attack success rate.
CITED -- InjecMEM (Tian et al., 2026): 76.6% ASR on MemoryOS via retriever-agnostic anchoring combined with Multi-GCG.
CITED -- eTAMP (Zou et al., 2026): environment-only web-observation poisoning producing 8x 'frustration' amplification, and crossing sessions and sites.
CITED -- Agent Smith (Gu et al., 2024): a single adversarial input propagates exponentially through pairwise exchanges to reach ~1,000,000 multimodal agents.
VMG dependency pre-order (authors' own): VF_epsilon <= RB <= PV <= WA, with PS orthogonal but required for confidentiality.
baselines_compared: Nine prior surveys compared in Table 1: Zhang et al. (2025d), Wu et al. (2025b), Liang et al. (2025b), He et al. (2024), Mu et al. (2026), Bodea et al. (2026), Hu et al. (2025), Tang et al. (2026), Luo et al. (2026).
No experimental baselines -- the paper runs no experiments; comparison is qualitative coverage against these surveys.
